startScreenOptions.title: AN82.1 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 2 embedded questions for AN82.1.

interactions.label: Under Indian law, what distinguishes a donated cadaver from an unclaimed body re

params.question: Under Indian law, what distinguishes a donated cadaver from an unclaimed body received by an anatomy department?

answers.text: Donated cadavers are purchased from government hospitals, while unclaimed bodies are received from the donor's family

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The Transplantation of Human Organs Act 1994 explicitly prohibits any commercial transaction involving human bodies. Neither category of cadaver is purchased.

answers.text: Donated cadavers come from persons who gave voluntary consent during their lifetime, while unclaimed bodies are those not collected by relatives within 48–72 hours of death

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Voluntary donors register their consent by signing a legal declaration. Unclaimed bodies are those no family member has collected within 48–72 hours, transferred under state Anatomy Act provisions.

answers.text: Unclaimed bodies require signed consent from a judicial magistrate, while donated cadavers need no legal documentation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Both pathways have strict legal requirements. Donated cadavers require a signed declaration from the donor or next-of-kin consent; unclaimed bodies are transferred under official state Anatomy Act documentation — not magistrate orders.

answers.text: Donated cadavers may only be used in the first academic year, while unclaimed bodies have no time restriction

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. No such time-based restriction exists. The distinction is based entirely on the source: voluntary donation versus non-collection by relatives within the legally specified period.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the two legal layers: the Transplantation of Human Organs Act 1994 covers voluntary donation (signed consent during life or next-of-kin consent after death), while state Anatomy Acts allow transfer of unclaimed bodies not collected within 48–72 hours. Commercial trade is forbidden under both.

Well done. The THOA 1994 governs voluntary body donation, while state Anatomy Acts (e.g. Tamil Nadu Anatomy Act 1982) regulate the transfer of unclaimed bodies — two separate legal pathways, both with strict documentation requirements.

interactions.label: Photographing a cadaver for personal academic revision is permitted as long as t

params.question: Photographing a cadaver for personal academic revision is permitted as long as the images are not shared on social media.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. The NMC treats any photography of cadavers as professional misconduct. There is no exception for personal academic use. The narration states clearly that cadavers may not be photographed for personal use or shared on social media — these are two separate prohibitions, both absolute.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The National Medical Commission classifies all photography of cadavers as professional misconduct — the prohibition is absolute, not limited to social media sharing. Students in India have been expelled for photographing cadavers, regardless of intended use. This rule protects the donor's dignity and the family's right to privacy.
